China Cultural Odyssey  ·  Spring 2026

THE SAME SONG, DIFFERENT
WORLDS

Political Meaning Across Regions in China

This project explores how political meaning shifts as we travel across different regions and provinces in China — where the same cultural works could become a memory of protest and threats, or nostalgia and comfort depending on our historical and geographic context.

Explore the Map View Case Studies
Scroll to explore
Introduction

PROJECT
OVERVIEW

Culture, Geography,
and Meaning

I
Research Question
How does political meaning shift?

How does political meaning shift as cultural works cross different regions and provinces in China?

II
Central Argument
Political meaning is not fixed.

As foreigners, it is common to view China as a singular, functioning entity — which has become the general perspective both the public and the government often place on the country. However, political meaning is not fixed. The same songs and cultural works develop entirely different meanings depending on geography, historical context, and personal experience.

III
Method & Sources
Regional case studies and memory

By compiling historical context, regional investigations and case studies, individual narratives, and personal memory, this project explores how different regions interact with the same cultural objects and ideas.

A Reflection
"

In general, politics can be a very sensitive and complicated subject
to touch on — yet, even ordinary cultural experiences can become
politically interpreted depending on context.

Growing up, my parents — originally from Fuzhou, China — would often play Chinese songs of all genres from their time in China; specifically, they would often find themselves playing songs by Cui Jian and Teresa Teng. The music of Teresa Teng and Cui Jian never felt political to me, nor were there any implications from my parents that they carried such a message — they were simply songs connected to memory, family, and everyday life.

Yet through this project, I began to realize how the same cultural works could carry entirely different meanings depending on where they were heard and who was listening. In some regions, these songs became associated with protest, ideological anxiety, and political resistance. In others, they remained deeply personal — tied instead to nostalgia, comfort, and emotional memory.

Geography did not merely shape how people experienced the music; it shaped what the music ultimately meant. By tracing these shifting interpretations across regions and political boundaries, this project explores how political meaning is often not fixed within the cultural object itself, but created through historical context, lived experience, and collective memory.

Binsen Lin  ·  China Cultural Odyssey  ·  Spring 2026

Cultural Cartography

REGIONS OF
INTERPRETATION

Explore how political meaning shifted across geographic and cultural boundaries in China. Each region developed distinct interpretations of the same cultural works depending on local history, memory, and political context.

← Hover or click a region to explore
Loading map…
Beijing
"Music as Protest"
Cultural Object
Cui Jian — Nothing to My Name
Interpretation
"In Beijing during the late 1980s, Cui Jian's music became associated with youth disillusionment, collective identity, and political protest during the Tiananmen movement."
Context
"As China's political center, Beijing became a space where music was more directly tied to public political expression and student activism."
Source
UCLA International Institute, China Daily, Tiananmen archival research
Zhejiang
"Music as Ideological Threat"
Cultural Object
Anti–'yellow music' campaigns
Interpretation
"In Zhejiang, local campaigns framed certain popular music as morally dangerous and ideologically threatening during the reform era."
Context
"Local governments organized cultural campaigns intended to distinguish revolutionary culture from 'decadent' or foreign influence."
Source
Cambridge research on anti-decadent music campaigns
Fujian
"Music as Memory"
Cultural Object
Teresa Teng — cassette circulation and family listening culture
Interpretation
"In Fujian, Teresa Teng's music often became associated with emotional memory, domestic listening spaces, and everyday life rather than overt political protest."
Context
"Due to Fujian's coastal geography and proximity to Taiwan, outside broadcasts and cassette circulation became more accessible during the reform era."
Source
Personal memory, Cold War broadcasting studies, cassette circulation research
Guangdong
"Music as Circulation"
Cultural Object
Radio broadcasts and cassette circulation
Interpretation
"In Guangdong, outside broadcasts, cassette tapes, and reform-era openness contributed to new forms of cultural circulation and interpretation."
Context
"As a southern coastal region with greater outside access, Guangdong became a key point of cultural exchange and media circulation."
Source
Reform-era media circulation research
Taiwan / Kinmen
"Music Across Borders"
Cultural Object
Teresa Teng — radio broadcasts across the strait
Interpretation
"Across Taiwan and Kinmen, Teresa Teng's music became intertwined with cross-strait broadcasting and Cold War cultural influence."
Context
"Broadcasts and transmitted music transformed popular songs into symbols of political and cultural influence across borders."
Source
Cold War broadcasting research, cross-strait media studies
Case Study

Cui Jian — Nothing to My Name

View All Regions →
The Cultural Object

Cultural Object /
Musical Experience

Album Cover
一无所有 · 1986
Live Performance
Concert Stage
Print Circulation
Media Landscape

Released during the emergence of Chinese rock in the 1980s, Cui Jian's Nothing to My Name introduced a raw and emotionally direct sound that contrasted sharply with the polished musical conventions of the time. Through live performances, cassette circulation, and underground listening spaces, the song became closely tied to youth music culture and the growing presence of rock as a new form of artistic expression in China. Its sparse instrumentation, distinctive vocals, and intimate lyrical style helped define the atmosphere of an entire musical era.

vs
The Political Meaning

Political Interpretation /
Regional Meaning

Tiananmen 1989
Protest March
Beijing Rock Scene
Underground Culture
Cassette Circulation
Underground Distribution

Across different regions and political contexts, "Nothing to My Name" accumulated entirely different meanings. In Beijing it was protest; in Fujian it was nostalgia; in Guangdong it circulated as subversive cassette culture. The same song became a vessel for whatever political and emotional reality surrounded it — protest, longing, memory, or threat.

"Rock music's gradual absorption into China's burgeoning market economy has defused much of its politically oppositional potential while allowing for the music to extend its cultural influence well beyond the narrow confines of the Beijing subculture from which it originally arose." — Andrew F. Jones
Experience the Song
